entries.title: Lipopolysaccharide endotoxins
entries.studied: Gram-negative bacteria broadly; lipid A structure, biosynthesis (Raetz pathway), transport, and TLR4 signaling
entries.finding: Lipid A biosynthesis follows the conserved nine-step Raetz pathway; lipid A-Kdo2 is the minimal LPS structure required for outer membrane biogenesis in most Gram-negatives. LpxC validated as an antibiotic target.
entries.notes: Foundational LPS review; establishes the lipid A essentiality paradigm that Caulobacter violates. Serves Introduction background.

entries.title: Caulobacter lipid A is conditionally dispensable in the absence of fur and in the presence of anionic sphingolipids
entries.studied: Caulobacter crescentus NA1000; Δfur, ΔlpxC, ΔctpA mutants; genetics, lipidomics, suppressor analysis
entries.finding: Mutations in fur allow C. crescentus to survive without LpxC or CtpA; viability requires anionic sphingolipid ceramide phosphoglycerate (CPG). 'Alterations in Fur-regulated processes, rather than iron status per se, underlie the ability to survive when lipid A synthesis is blocked.'
entries.notes: Keystone paper for the entire throughline. Establishes the Δfur + sphingolipid mechanism. Ryan lab collaboration source.

entries.title: Homologues of the inner-membrane LPS transport proteins are required for sphingolipid transport in Caulobacter crescentus
entries.studied: C. crescentus lipid-A-deficient strains; inner membrane lipid transport complex
entries.finding: Homologues of inner-membrane LPS transport proteins (Lpt paralogs) are required for sphingolipid transport in lipid-A-deficient Caulobacter; identifies shared Lpt machinery components for CPG trafficking.
entries.notes: Directly supports throughline sub-claim on Lpt apparatus repurposing for sphingolipid transport. Most recent Klein lab follow-up.

entries.title: Characterization of an evolutionarily distinct bacterial ceramide kinase from Caulobacter crescentus
entries.studied: C. crescentus CpgB recombinant protein; enzyme kinetics in vitro
entries.finding: CpgB is an evolutionarily distinct bacterial ceramide kinase: Km = 19.2 +/- 5.5 uM for NBD C6-ceramide, Vmax = 2590 +/- 230 pmol/min/mg. Phylogenetically distinct from eukaryotic ceramide kinases; NVP-231 inhibitor has no effect.
entries.notes: First step in CPG head-group biosynthesis. Supports constitutive sphingolipid biosynthesis sub-claim.

entries.title: CpgD is a phosphoglycerate cytidylyltransferase required for ceramide diphosphoglycerate synthesis
entries.studied: C. crescentus CpgD (CCNA_01210) recombinant protein; enzyme kinetics in vitro
entries.finding: CpgD is a phosphoglycerate cytidylyltransferase producing CDP-glycerate from CTP and 3-phosphoglycerate (Km,app = 10.9 +/- 0.7 mM for 3-PG); required for ceramide diphosphoglycerate (CPG2) synthesis.
entries.notes: PubMed-indexed as preprint (bioRxiv). Supports constitutive sphingolipid biosynthesis sub-claim.

entries.title: Five structural genes required for ceramide synthesis in Caulobacter and for bacterial survival
entries.studied: C. crescentus NA1000; Δspt and cofitness-linked mutants; lipid analysis, growth/survival assays
entries.finding: Five structural genes required for dihydroceramide biosynthesis; sphingolipid biosynthesis needed for stationary-phase survival and OM integrity. Δspt mutant has much reduced survival at elevated temperatures.
entries.notes: Establishes the ceramide biosynthetic pathway including spt. Supports constitutive sphingolipid biosynthesis sub-claim (evidence map row 6).

entries.title: Genes required for phosphosphingolipid formation in Caulobacter crescentus contribute to bacterial virulence
entries.studied: C. crescentus NA1000; 11 sphingolipid biosynthesis genes; polymyxin B sensitivity; Galleria mellonella virulence model
entries.finding: At least 11 genes participate in phosphosphingolipid formation; all required for membrane stability and polymyxin B sensitivity. Sphingolipid genes contribute to C. crescentus virulence on G. mellonella.
entries.notes: Extends sphingolipid pathway from 5 to 11 genes. Supports constitutive sphingolipid biosynthesis sub-claim.

entries.title: mSphere of Influence: Celebrating exceptions to the rule of lipid A essentiality
entries.studied: Comparative review of A. baumannii (Boll lab, PG-remodeling route) and C. crescentus (Zik/Ryan, fur + sphingolipid route) lipid A dispensability
entries.finding: Exceptions to lipid A essentiality illuminate distinct bacterial strategies: A. baumannii uses PBP1A loss + ld-transpeptidase PG remodeling, while C. crescentus relies on Fur derepression + anionic sphingolipid substitution.
entries.notes: Commentary framing piece; directly compares the two known lipid A dispensability routes. Serves Introduction + Discussion context.

entries.title: Control of lipopolysaccharide biosynthesis by FtsH-mediated proteolysis of LpxC is conserved in enterobacteria but not in all gram-negative bacteria
entries.studied: E. coli, Salmonella, Yersinia, Vibrio, Agrobacterium tumefaciens, Rhodobacter capsulatus, Pseudomonas aeruginosa; FtsH-mediated LpxC proteolysis
entries.finding: FtsH-mediated LpxC proteolysis is conserved in Enterobacteriaceae but NOT in all Gram-negatives. A. tumefaciens and R. capsulatus LpxC degraded by Lon protease; P. aeruginosa LpxC is stable. Alphaproteobacteria may use non-FtsH mechanisms.
entries.notes: Relevant to why LpxC dynamics in Caulobacter differ from E. coli. Supports species-specificity sub-claim.

entries.title: Iron Deficiency Generates Oxidative Stress and Activation of the SOS Response in Caulobacter crescentus
entries.studied: C. crescentus NA1000 wild type under iron limitation (2,2'-dipyridyl) and Δfur mutant; RNA-seq
entries.finding: Identified 256 upregulated and 236 downregulated genes under iron limitation; 176 and 204 newly identified respectively. Sixteen transcription factors and seven sRNAs upregulated. Iron deficiency causes endogenous oxidative stress and SOS response activation.
entries.notes: Primary reference for Caulobacter Fur regulon RNA-seq. Directly used in NB01 for Leaden DEG set comparison (evidence map row 3).

entries.title: Fur controls iron homeostasis and oxidative stress defense in the oligotrophic alpha-proteobacterium Caulobacter crescentus
entries.studied: C. crescentus NA1000 Δfur mutant; ChIP + microarray + bioinformatics for Fur-binding sites
entries.finding: Fur functions as both activator and repressor, integrating iron metabolism and oxidative stress response. Iron uptake genes repressed by iron-Fur and induced under iron limitation; iron-using protein genes activated by Fur under iron sufficiency.
entries.notes: Earlier ChIP/microarray Caulobacter Fur regulon paper; frequently cited by the Klein/Ryan group. Supports Fur-released transport sub-claim (evidence map row 1).

entries.title: Mapping the IscR regulon sheds light on the regulation of iron homeostasis in Caulobacter crescentus
entries.studied: C. crescentus NA1000 ΔiscR mutant; RNA-seq + ChIP-seq; 302-gene IscR regulon
entries.finding: IscR regulon comprises 302 genes with partial Fur overlap. IscR represses the respiration regulator FixK, fine-tuning gene regulation in response to iron and redox balance. Two distinct IscR binding motifs identified.
entries.notes: IscR/Fur overlap relevant to partitioning Fur-specific vs broader iron-regulon contributions. Supports evidence map row 1.

entries.title: A co-conserved gene pair supports Caulobacter iron homeostasis during chelation stress
entries.studied: C. crescentus NA1000; RB-TnSeq under EDTA chelation; CciT (TBDT) and CciO dioxygenase
entries.finding: CciT is a Fur-regulated TonB-dependent transporter that supports iron homeostasis during chelation stress; strictly requires co-conserved CciO (Fe2+ dioxygenase). Gene pair broadly co-conserved in Proteobacteria.
entries.notes: Defines a specific Fur-regulated TBDT pair in Caulobacter. Supports Fur-released transport sub-claim (evidence map row 1).

entries.title: Role and regulation of ferritin-like proteins in iron homeostasis and oxidative stress survival of Caulobacter crescentus
entries.studied: C. crescentus NA1000; Bfr, Dps, CC_0557 ferritin-like proteins; Δbfr, Δdps mutants
entries.finding: bfr expression positively regulated (2.5-fold) by Fe-Fur. Δdps very sensitive to H2O2; Bfr contributes to iron homeostasis, Dps to oxidative stress protection. Ferritins link Fur regulation to iron storage.
entries.notes: Caulobacter-specific Fur-regulated iron storage. Supports Fur regulon context (evidence map row 1).

entries.title: TonB-Dependent Heme/Hemoglobin Utilization by Caulobacter crescentus HutA
entries.studied: C. crescentus NA1000; 62 putative TBDTs; iron transport kinetics; sarcosinate extracts + mass spectrometry
entries.finding: Identified five TBDTs with regulated expression: three overexpressed during iron deficiency (CCNA_00028, 02277, 03023), two during iron repletion. HutA (CCNA_02277) is the first characterized C. crescentus heme/hemoglobin transporter.
entries.notes: Fur-regulated TBDTs in Caulobacter; relevant to the n=32 TBDT subset derepressed in Δfur (evidence map row 1).

entries.title: OxyR and the hydrogen peroxide stress response in Caulobacter crescentus
entries.studied: C. crescentus NA1000 wild type and ΔoxyR; H2O2 exposure at two time points; RNA-seq
entries.finding: Fur and part of its regulon were differentially expressed in response to H2O2 independently of OxyR. OxyR required for expression of 103 genes in H2O2 response. Demonstrates Fur-OxyR cross-regulation under oxidative stress.
entries.notes: Fur cross-talk with oxidative stress response; relevant to envelope stress phenotypes in the TBDT subset (evidence map row 1).

entries.title: The ChvG-ChvI and NtrY-NtrX Two-Component Systems Coordinately Regulate Growth of Caulobacter crescentus
entries.studied: C. crescentus NA1000; ΔchvG, ΔchvI, ΔntrY, ΔntrX mutants; RNA-seq for ChvI regulon; genetic suppressor analysis
entries.finding: Defined the ChvI transcriptional regulon in C. crescentus; significant overlap with NtrX regulon. chvGI deletion limits growth in defined medium. NtrZ identified as periplasmic inhibitor of NtrY phosphatase activity.
entries.notes: Primary ChvI regulon reference; used in NB03 for ChvI two-phase partition (evidence map row 4). Dataset in clean/chvi_overexpression_regulon_stein_2021.csv.

entries.title: The two-component system ChvGI maintains cell envelope homeostasis in Caulobacter crescentus
entries.studied: C. crescentus NA1000; chv mutants under vancomycin + osmotic stress; ChvI regulon via RNA-seq
entries.finding: ChvI regulates cell envelope architecture controlling OM, PG assembly/recycling, and IM proteins. ChvI phosphorylation activated by vancomycin. ChvGI TCS monitors osmotic imbalances and PG damage to maintain envelope homeostasis.
entries.notes: Independent ChvI regulon confirmation; dataset in clean/chvi_regulon_quintero_yanes_2022.csv. Supports evidence map row 4.

entries.title: The ChvG-ChvI Regulatory Network: A Conserved Global Regulatory Circuit Among the Alphaproteobacteria with Pervasive Impacts on Host Interactions and Diverse Cellular Processes
entries.studied: Alphaproteobacteria broadly; ChvG-ChvI conservation, ExoR regulation, regulon targets across species
entries.finding: ChvG-ChvI is a conserved global regulatory circuit in alphaproteobacteria controlling envelope composition, virulence, biofilm, motility. Acidic pH stimulates ExoR proteolysis, activating ChvG-ChvI. Envelope stress may also generally activate.
entries.notes: Authoritative synthesis of ChvG-ChvI biology. Conceptual scaffold for ChvI two-phase signaling sub-claim (evidence map row 4).

entries.title: Primary role of the Tol-Pal complex in bacterial outer membrane lipid homeostasis
entries.studied: E. coli; chimeric Tol-Pal complex engineered to lose septal enrichment but retain OM function
entries.finding: Primary function of Tol-Pal complex is OM lipid homeostasis (retrograde phospholipid transport), independent of its role during division. Peripherally-localized Tol-Pal fully capable of maintaining OM lipid balance.
entries.notes: Basis for revised Pal-upregulation interpretation in Discussion (evidence map row 8). E. coli system, not Caulobacter.

entries.title: The caulobacter Tol-Pal complex is essential for outer membrane integrity and the positioning of a polar localization factor
entries.studied: C. crescentus; TolA, TolB, Pal depletion/mutant strains; cryo-EM, fluorescence microscopy
entries.finding: Caulobacter Tol-Pal complex is essential for OM integrity and cell division (unlike E. coli where dispensable). Components localize to division plane; Pal interacts with TipN polar localization factor.
entries.notes: Caulobacter-specific Tol-Pal essentiality context. Pal upregulation in Δfur must be interpreted against this essential-complex background (evidence map row 8).

entries.title: Septal Class A Penicillin-Binding Protein Activity and ld-Transpeptidases Mediate Selection of Colistin-Resistant Lipooligosaccharide-Deficient Acinetobacter baumannii
entries.studied: A. baumannii ATCC 19606; PBP1A, LdtJ, LdtK mutants; colistin-resistant LOS-deficient isolates; transposon mutagenesis
entries.finding: PBP1A inactivation is a compensatory mutation supporting LOS-deficient A. baumannii isolation. Both LdtJ and LdtK ld-transpeptidases required for LOS-deficient colony selection. PG remodeling is the tolerance route, NOT Fur.
entries.notes: Direct A. baumannii comparator: PG-remodeling route vs Caulobacter sphingolipid route. Supports cross-species pathway absence sub-claim (evidence map row 9).

entries.title: Outer membrane composition of a lipopolysaccharide-deficient Neisseria meningitidis mutant
entries.studied: N. meningitidis; ΔlpxA mutant; OM composition, phospholipid analysis, capsule requirement
entries.finding: Viable lpxA-null N. meningitidis; OMP assembly largely unaffected but iron-limitation-inducible lipoprotein expression greatly reduced. Capsular polysaccharide essential for viability without LPS. PL composition shifted to shorter-chain saturated fatty acids.
entries.notes: N. meningitidis cross-species comparator: capsule-dependent route, not Fur/sphingolipid. Supports evidence map row 9.

entries.title: Identification of two late acyltransferase genes responsible for lipid A biosynthesis in Moraxella catarrhalis
entries.studied: M. catarrhalis O35E; ΔlpxX, ΔlpxL single mutants; lipid A structural analysis, serum sensitivity, mouse model
entries.finding: Identified lpxX (decanoyl transferase) and lpxL (dodecanoyl transferase) for lipid A biosynthesis. ΔlpxX sensitive to serum and hydrophobic reagents with reduced growth; ΔlpxL largely unaffected.
entries.notes: M. catarrhalis cross-species comparator: late acyltransferase mutants viable without Fur involvement. Supports evidence map row 9.

entries.title: Late acyltransferase genes lpxX and lpxL jointly contribute to the biological activities of Moraxella catarrhalis
entries.studied: M. catarrhalis O35E; ΔlpxXΔlpxL double mutant; lipid A analysis, serum resistance, mouse pulmonary clearance
entries.finding: Double mutant ΔlpxXL lacks three secondary acyl chains from lipid A; dramatically reduced endotoxin content, reduced serum resistance, and accelerated bacterial clearance at 0, 3, and 6 h in mouse model.
entries.notes: M. catarrhalis double mutant comparator. Supports evidence map row 9.

entries.title: Colistin resistance in Acinetobacter baumannii is mediated by complete loss of lipopolysaccharide production
entries.studied: A. baumannii ATCC 19606; 13 independent colistin-resistant derivatives; lpxA, lpxC, lpxD mutations
entries.finding: A. baumannii can develop colistin resistance by complete loss of LPS production via mutations in lpxA, lpxC, or lpxD. First report of spontaneously occurring LPS-deficient Gram-negative bacterium.
entries.notes: Original A. baumannii LPS-loss demonstration. Cross-species comparator (no Fur). Supports evidence map row 9.

entries.title: Insertion sequence ISAba11 is involved in colistin resistance and loss of lipopolysaccharide in Acinetobacter baumannii
entries.studied: A. baumannii clinical isolates; ISAba11 insertion sequence in lpxA and lpxC
entries.finding: ISAba11 insertion sequence movement inactivates lpxA and lpxC in A. baumannii, causing complete LPS loss and high-level colistin resistance in clinical isolates.
entries.notes: IS-driven mechanism in clinical A. baumannii. Supports evidence map row 9 (alternative LPS-loss route without Fur).

entries.title: Lessons from an LPS-deficient Neisseria meningitidis mutant
entries.studied: N. meningitidis ΔlpxA; review of OM composition, immunogenicity, and inflammatory response
entries.finding: LPS-deficient N. meningitidis retains OMP assembly but has reduced iron-limitation-inducible lipoprotein expression and reduced OM protein immunogenicity. Capsule essential for viability without LPS.
entries.notes: Companion review to Steeghs 2001. Notes reduced iron-limitation-inducible lipoproteins (oblique iron connection). Supports evidence map row 9.

entries.title: Modulating substrate choice: the SspB adaptor delivers a regulator of the extracytoplasmic-stress response to the AAA+ protease ClpXP for degradation
entries.studied: E. coli; SspB adaptor, ClpXP protease, RseA anti-sigma-E factor; trapping + biochemical + genetic studies
entries.finding: SspB delivers RseA (anti-sigma-E) to ClpXP for degradation, directly coupling SspB to envelope-stress (sigma-E) signaling. ClpX and SspB participate in induction of the sigma-E regulon in vivo.
entries.notes: Foundational SspB biology in E. coli. Relevant because project strains carry ΔsspB (evidence map rows 2-3). Does this logic apply in Caulobacter?

entries.title: Identification of ClpP substrates in Caulobacter crescentus reveals a role for regulated proteolysis in bacterial development
entries.studied: C. crescentus; ClpP substrate trapping + biochemical validation; TacA, CtrA, GcrA, SocB substrates
entries.finding: Identified and validated ClpXP substrates in C. crescentus including TacA (stalk synthesis TF), CtrA, GcrA, SocB. Regulated proteolysis critical for cell cycle progression and Caulobacter development.
entries.notes: Caulobacter-direct ClpP substrate catalog. SspB is in the substrate set. Supports SspB/ClpXP context (evidence map rows 2-3).

entries.title: Versatile modes of peptide recognition by the ClpX N domain mediate alternative adaptor-binding specificities in different bacterial species
entries.studied: E. coli and C. crescentus SspB-alpha; ClpX N domain recognition; cross-species substrate delivery
entries.finding: E. coli SspB can deliver substrates to Caulobacter ClpXP despite very limited sequence homology between SspB orthologs. ClpX N domain is a versatile platform for peptide recognition with alternative adaptor-binding specificities.
entries.notes: Explicitly compares E. coli and C. crescentus SspB; confirms functional conservation of adaptor-protease coupling across species (evidence map rows 2-3).

entries.title: Functional Interaction between the Cytoplasmic ABC Protein LptB and the Inner Membrane LptC Protein, Components of the Lipopolysaccharide Transport Machinery in Escherichia coli
entries.studied: E. coli; LptBCFG complex; LptB-LptC functional interaction; LptC truncation mutants; Pa-LptC chimera
entries.finding: LptB and LptC functionally interact; LptB overexpression suppresses lethality of C-terminal LptC deletions and supports hybrid Lpt machinery carrying P. aeruginosa LptC. LptB key for Lpt machine assembly.
entries.notes: E. coli Lpt machinery context. Relevant to interpreting Caulobacter MsbA-like/LptC-related transcript upregulation (evidence map row 7).

entries.title: Mutant phenotypes for thousands of bacterial genes of unknown function
entries.studied: 32 phylogenetically diverse bacteria including C. crescentus; RB-TnSeq across dozens of growth conditions
entries.finding: Identified mutant phenotypes for 11,779 protein-coding genes lacking functional annotation; 2,316 conserved high-confidence associations. Demonstrates scalability of microbial genetics for improving gene annotations.
entries.notes: Methods provenance: primary citation for RB-TnSeq methodology and BERDL kescience_fitnessbrowser data source used in NB02/NB02b.

entries.title: PaperBLAST: Text Mining Papers for Information about Homologs
entries.studied: Text mining of >700,000 scientific articles linked to >400,000 protein sequences via EuropePMC + curated databases
entries.finding: PaperBLAST rapidly finds similar proteins discussed in literature and presents text snippets from relevant articles. Links EuropePMC full-text search with Swiss-Prot, GeneRIF, and EcoCyc curated annotations.
entries.notes: Methods provenance: primary citation for PaperBLAST (BERDL kescience_paperblast). Used in NB06 cross-species analysis. REPORT.md PMID was wrong (33531404); correct is 28845458.

entries.title: KBase: The United States Department of Energy Systems Biology Knowledgebase
entries.studied: KBase systems biology knowledgebase platform; integrated tools for genome assembly, annotation, metabolic modeling, multi-omics analysis
entries.finding: KBase is the DOE Systems Biology Knowledgebase providing integrated computational tools for systems biology research; enables reproducible, collaborative genome-scale data analysis.
entries.notes: Methods provenance: primary citation for KBase infrastructure. BERDL is built on KBase.

entries.title: edgeR: a Bioconductor package for differential expression analysis of digital gene expression data
entries.studied: Computational methodology for differential expression analysis of replicated count data (RNA-seq, DGE)
entries.finding: edgeR uses an overdispersed Poisson model with empirical Bayes moderation of dispersion across transcripts to analyze differential expression of digital gene expression data; works with minimal replication levels.
entries.notes: Methods provenance: edgeR used for DE analysis in project notebooks (methods_provenance.md lists edgeR as statistical test).

entries.title: Antimicrobial Agents That Inhibit the Outer Membrane Assembly Machines of Gram-Negative Bacteria
entries.studied: Gram-negative bacteria broadly; Lol (lipoprotein), BAM (OMP), and Lpt (LPS) OM assembly pathways; inhibitor development
entries.finding: Reviews OM component transport machinery (Lol for lipoproteins, BAM for OMPs, Lpt for LPS) and development of inhibitors targeting these systems. OM assembly machines are potential novel antibiotic targets.
entries.notes: Background review of OM assembly machines; provides context for Lpt pathway repurposing in Caulobacter (evidence map row 7).
